coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Bi/BiOCl-TiO2-CQDs quaternary photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and fast charge migration
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wei
Zhao, Chenhui
Zhang, Qiuyu
coredata.dc:description: A series of Bi/BiOCl/TiO2-CQDs(α) (BBTC-α) quaternary photocatalysts with lamellar structure were synthesized via a solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. The BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst exhibited highest visible-light photoactivity in decomposing methyl orange and p-nitrophenol, and it was about 4.8 and 10.3 times of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets. In this system, Bi and CQDs were served as electron donors, and BiOCl and TiO2 were served as electron trappers to extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. The enhanced visible-light photoactivity could be attributed to the strong light absorption and upconversion photoluminescence of the CQDs, and the surface plasmon resonance effect of the surface Bi also played an important role in this system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodegradation
Organic pollutant
Carbon quantum dots
Visible-light
Plasmonic effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets (a) and BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst (b). The inset of b is the corresponding FFT image.
XRD patterns of anatase TiO2 (a), tetragonal BiOCl (b), BiOCl/TiO2 (c), and BBTC-0.6 (d).

Fitted Langmuir-Hinshelwood models of MO (a) and PNP (b) by the photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation. The insets in panel a and b are the corresponding rate constants of BiOCl/TiO2 and BBTC-0.6 in the MO degradation and PNP degradation, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the proposed photocatalystic mechanism of the BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst under visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Bi/BiOCl-TiO2-CQDs quaternary photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and fast charge migration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Weiyang Road, Xi'an, Shanxi 710021, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of Bi/BiOCl/TiO2-CQDs(α) (BBTC-α) quaternary photocatalysts with lamellar structure were synthesized via a solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. The BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst exhibited highest visible-light photoactivity in decomposing methyl orange and p-nitrophenol, and it was about 4.8 and 10.3 times of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets. In this system, Bi and CQDs were served as electron donors, and BiOCl and TiO2 were served as electron trappers to extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. The enhanced visible-light photoactivity could be attributed to the strong light absorption and upconversion photoluminescence of the CQDs, and the surface plasmon resonance effect of the surface Bi also played an important role in this system.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the simplest bismuth based compounds, bismuthoxyhalide-family semiconductor materials (BiOX, X=Cl, Br, I) have attracted much attention due to their high stability, non-toxicity, suitable band gaps and superior photoactivity [1,2]. For instance, Dong et al. [3] investigated the change from nonselective oxidation to selective oxidation in the NO removal on BiOI surface. Zhang et al. [4] studied the oxygen vacancy structure associated photocatalytic water oxidation of BiOCl. Zhao et al. [5] first used the reactable polyelectrolyte, poly(allylamine hydrochloride) to fabricate BiOCl materials via an assisted solvothermal method. However, among the BiOX semiconductors, the photoactivity of the pure BiOCl is limited by its low light absorption efficiency, difficult charge migration and fast recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. To improve its photocatalytic performance, many methods had been attempted, such as the preparation of heterojunctions [6,7], introduction of SPR effect [8,9], crystal plane controlling [10,11], metal doping or nonmetal doping [12,13]. For instance, Liu et al. [6] explored a group of BiO(ClBr)(1−x)/2Ix solid solution with a homogeneous layered tetragonal matlockite structure for visible-light photodegradation of 2-propanol. Rochkind et al. [14] used the synthetic derivatives of flavins as photosensitizers to facilitate the heterogeneous photocatalytic activity of BiOCl particles under visible light by covalently attaching on its surface. Zhang et al. [15] synthesized the BiOCl/BiOBr composite photocatalysts with different Cl-to-Br molar ratios by a simple microwave-assisted coprecipitation method. Obviously, aforementioned modifications to the BiOCl semiconductor could indeed improve its visible-light photocatalytic performance.

CQDs, known as a novel class of recently discovered carbon-based materials, exhibit strong visible-light absorption and upconversion photoluminescence, are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions [16]. Due to its representative conjugated π structure, CQDs exhibits the excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties, and open a new way for efficiently utilizing the full spectrum [17,18]. Therefore, the investigation of CQDs in photocatalysis field has attracted many researchers in recent years. For instance, Di et al. [19] prepared a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified atomically-thin BiOI nanosheet nanojunctions. Liu et al. [20] developed a carbon quantum dots-decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst with whole spectrum response. Chen et al. [21] proposed a practical strategy to facilitate the separation of e
−
-h
+ pairs and enhance the photostability in a semiconductor by the use of a Schottky junction in a noble metal-CQDs-semiconductor stack structure. Fang et al. [22] synthesized the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets coupled with carbon nanodots as 2D/0D composites for efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution. No doubt, the CQDs indeed exhibit prominent superiority for improving the photocatalytic performance of traditional photocatalysts, and is a desired candidate for the modification of other photocatalysts.

In our previous research [23], the BiOCl/TiO2 composite with lamellar structure was found could be synthesized by a facile one-pot solvothermal method when the proportion of titanium source was appropriate. For the formation of the BiOCl-TiO2 heterojunction, the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets exhibited higher visible-light photoactivity than the pure BiOCl and TiO2. However, the its photocatalytic performance should be further improved if being used to decompose the hard-degradable organic pollutant in visible-light range. Therefore, some superior BiOCl/TiO2-based ternary composites had been reported by some researchers, and enhanced performance had indeed been obtained, such as the synthesis of the BiOCl/Bi5Nb3O15/TiO2, attapulgite-BiOCl-TiO2 and TiO2/Bi2(BDC)3/BiOCl composites [24–26]. Inspired by other researchers' discoveries, we aim to further improve the visible-light photocatalytic performance of our synthesized BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets by the CQDs loading. As known to all of us, the lamellar structure of the BiOCl/TiO2 composite would be very conducive to the light absorption for the exposed effective absorption surface, and the CQDs with strong light absorption and upconversion photoluminescence can effectively improve the visible-light response of the BiOCl/TiO2 composite. Besides, the rhombohedral Bi as co-catalyst was found to be generated in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix during the followed hydrothermal process, thus the SPR effect would be introduced to this composite. As known to all of us, the SPR effect had been applied by many researchers in photocatalysis field [27,28]. However, no existing report is about the synthesis of the BBTC quaternary composites. Therefore, the structural features and photocatalytic performances of the photocatalysts were invesigated systematically.
The detailed experimental process is described in the Supporting Information (SI).

The morphology and microstructure of the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets and BBTC-α hybrid materials were characterized by SEM and TEM, and the corresponding images were shown in Fig. S1. The synthesized BiOCl/TiO2 composites exhibit the representative lamellar structure (Fig. S1a), and the TiO2 nanoclusters with the size of about 5–10nm are uniformly dispersed into the BiOCl matrix (Fig. S1b). At the same time, the obvious crystal lattices with the lattice spacing of 0.34nm, 0.26nm and 0.27nm can be observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1a), which can be attributed to the (101), (110) and (102) plane of tetragonal BiOCl, respectively [6,11,29]. Beyond that, the crystal lattices with the lattice spacing of 0.35nm can also be observed, which can be attributed to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [30]. After the CQDs loading, the surface roughness of the sample is increased significantly (Figs. S1d and S2d, g, m, p), and plenty of dark spots with the size of about 3–6nm can be observed clearly on the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix (Fig. S1e). As shown as the HRTEM images in Fig. 1b, the obvious crystal lattices with the lattice spacing of 0.32nm can be observed, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of CQDs [31]. In addition, the crystal lattices with the lattice spacing of 0.328nm can also be observed, which can be attributed to the (012) plane of rhombohedral Bi [6]. As shown as the inset in Fig. S1e, the electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the corresponding selected area indicates that the sample is polycrystalline, and the corresponding FFT image in Fig. 1b also indicates the polycrystalline characteristic, which confirms the successful preparation of the BBTC photocatalyst. The distinct multiple diffraction rings can be indexed with the anatase TiO2 and rhombohedral Bi. The change of the morphologies and the elementary composition with the change of the CQDs contents was discussed detailedly in the SI. Furthermore, the EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S3) also confirmed the existence of Bi, Ti, O, Cl and C elements in the BBTC-0.6, which proved the successful synthesis of the quaternary photocatalyst.

Subsequently, the crystalline structure of the samples were examined by XRD analysis. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the diffraction peaks corresponding to the tetragonal BiOCl (JCPDS 06-0249) [32] and anatase TiO2 (JCPDS 21-1272) [33] can be observed clearly by comparing with Fig. 2a and b. For the formation of the BiOCl-TiO2 hierarchical structure, the diffraction peak corresponding to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 shifted to 24.5° from 25.5°. After the CQDs loading (Fig. 2d), a wide diffraction peak corresponding to the amorphous carbon shell of CQDs can be observed around 28°, and no other signal about CQDs can be detected for its low content and weak diffraction. This result matches with other researchers' conclusion [21]. Beyond that, the new intense diffraction peaks located at about 27.5° and 30.6° can be assigned to the (012) and (102) plane of rhombohedral Bi (JCPDS 44-1246) [6], indicating that the rhombohedral phase Bi was generated in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix during the followed hydrothermal process.

FTIR analysis was carried out to further investigate the samples. As shown as Fig. S7a, the absorption peaks at 3399cm−1, 645cm−1 and 480cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic absorption of –OH stretching vibration, BiO stretching vibration and TiO bending vibration, respectively. The absorption peaks at 2922cm−1 and 2865cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic absorption of CH stretching vibration in methyl groups and methylene groups, and the absorption peaks at 1652cm−1 and 1283cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic absorption of CO and CN stretching vibration in PVP adsorbed on the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix. After the CQDs loading (Fig. S7b), the characteristic peak of OH stretching vibration shifts to 3431cm−1, indicating the presence of carboxyl groups. Furthermore, a new strong characteristic peaks around 1387cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C bond originating from the CQDs on the surface of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets. In addition, the characteristic peaks of the CH stretching vibration shift to the low wavenumbers in the FTIR spectrum of BBTC-0.6 hybrids, indicating the existence of the interaction between the interfaces of CQDs and BiOCl/TiO2 matrix. To further demonstrate this conclusion, the TG analysis was discussed in SI (Fig. S9).

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets and BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst were analyzed in SI (Fig. S10a). After the CQDs loading, both the BET surface area and pore volume increase significantly. Furthermore, the pore size distribution in Fig. S10b indicates that the mesopores had been formed in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix after the followed hydrothermal process. It can be attributed to the conversion of part BiOCl to Bi in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix. Apparently, the increased BET surface area would be very conducive to the improvement of the catalytic performance for the increased active sites and enhanced adsorption ability.

The surface chemical composition and valence state of the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets and BBTC-0.6 composite were analyzed by XPS analysis. As shown as Fig. S11a, both the survey spectra show the O, Ti, C, Cl and Bi signals. As displayed in Fig. S11b, for the interaction between the interfaces of BiOCl and TiO2, the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks in the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets were located at 161.5eV and 156.2eV, respectively. After the CQDs loading, the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks in BiOCl shifted to 161.3eV and 156.0eV, and the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks of Bi0 can be observed at 162.3eV and 157.1eV, indicating the generation of the elemental Bi in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix. Similarly, for the interaction between the interfaces of BiOCl and TiO2, the Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 peaks in the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets were located at 462.8eV and 456.0eV (Fig. S11c), respectively. Both the Bi 4f and Ti 2p peaks in the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets have a shift by comparing with the standard peak position [9,34], indicating that the BiTi interactions in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix indeed exist. After the CQDs loading, the further shift of the Bi 4f and Ti 2p signals indicates the existence of the BiC and TiC interactions [35], and three O 1s peaks also shifted to 529.8eV, 528.1eV and 527.2eV from 529.3eV, 528.3eV and 527.1eV (Fig. S11d), respectively. However, the signal intensity of Cl 2p peaks was decreased significantly (Fig. S11e). It should be caused by the CQDs covering and the conversion of surface BiOCl to elemental Bi in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix. Especially, the variation of the peak position of C 1s signal is very obvious after the CQDs loading (Fig. S11f). The C 1s peaks from PVP were weakened significantly, and the C 1s peak located at 285.0eV can be assigned to the CC bond with sp2 orbital in CQDs [21], which also demonstrates the leaching of PVP and existence of CQDs in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix.

Furthermore, the samples were analyzed by DRS spectra, EIS Nyquist plot and PL spectra (Fig. S12, S13 and S14). The BBTC-0.6 hybrid exhibits strongest visible-light response, smallest charge migration resistance and faster interfacial charge transfer ability than BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets, BiOCl and anatase TiO2, indicating that the appropriate CQDs introduction can effectively improve the light absorption ability and interfacial charge migration of the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix for the existence of strong photo-response of CQDs and the SPR effect of elemental Bi.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized BBTC-α photocatalysts were evaluated by the MO and PNP degradation in water system under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 3 ). Among them, the BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst exhibited the highest photoactivity (k=2.5×10−2 min−1) (Fig. 3a). About 94% of MO could be removed during 100min (Fig. S15a), and it was about 4.8 times of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets (5.2×10−3 min−1). The similar phenomenon could be observed in the PNP photodegradation, the BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst displayed the highest photoactivity (k=6.6×10−3 min−1) (Fig. 3b). About 82% of PNP could be removed during 240min (Fig. S15c), and it was about 10.3 times of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets (6.4×10−4 min−1). It demonstrated that the appropriate CQDs loading can significantly enhance the photoactivity of the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets for the upconversion PL property of CQDs and SPR effect of the elemental Bi in the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix, and too much or too little CQDs would be not conducive to the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets. The recycling experiments of the photodegradation of MO and PNP by the BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst indicate that the photocatalyst exhibits the absolute photostability and superior reusability (Fig. S17). In addition, the photoexcited e
− , h
+ and O 2 ·− radicals were demonstrated to be the main active specives in the photodegradation of MO and PNP in this photocatalytic system (Fig. S18).

The proposed photocatalytic mechanism of the BBTC-0.6 quaternary system under visible-light irradiation was displayed in Fig. 4 . In this architecture, both Bi co-catalyst and CQDs were served as electron donors, and both BiOCl and TiO2 were served as electron trappers to extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. Simultaneously, the CQDs can also be acted as the electron sink in the system, which can further improve the photogenerated electron migration ability of the photocatalyst. Detailedly, the CQDs were firstly excited under visible-light irradiation (λ 1) for its strong visible-light response property, and photoexcited e
− and h
+ were firstly produced. When the particle size of the CQDs is small enough, the upconversion photoluminescence would happen, and the light with little wavelength (λ 2) would be emitted, so that the BiOCl/TiO2 heterojunction would be excited. At the same time, the photoexcited e
− would also be produced by the Bi co-catalyst. Then, the photoexcited e
− would quickly transfer to the conduction band (CB) of BiOCl and TiO2 from the Bi co-catalyst and the CB of CQDs through their interfaces, and the transfer of photoexcited e
− from the CB of TiO2 to the CB of BiOCl may also happen. At the same time, the O2 molecules would react with the photoexcited e
− to generate O 2 ·− active radicals. Furthermore, the photoexcited h
+ would quickly transfer to the valence band (VB) of CQDs from the VB of BiOCl and TiO2 through the their interfaces, and the transfer of photoexcited h
+ from the VB of BiOCl to the VB of TiO2 may also happen. Finally, the organic pollutant molecules (MO or PNP) would react with photoexcited h
+ and O 2 ·− active radicals to generate the ultimate degradation production. Therefore, in this photocatalytic process, both the upconversion photoluminescence of CQDs and the SPR effect of Bi co-catalyst play the important role for improving the photocatalytic performance of the BiOCl/TiO2 matrix.

In this paper, a series of BBTC-α quaternary photocatalysts with lamellar structure were firstly synthesized via a solvothermal method followed by a hydrothermal process. The BBTC-0.6 photocatalyst exhibited highest visible-light photoactivity in decomposing methyl orange and p-nitrophenol, and it was about 4.8 and 10.3 times of BiOCl/TiO2 nanosheets. In this system, Bi and CQDs were served as electron donors, and BiOCl and TiO2 were served as electron trappers to extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. The enhanced visible-light photoactivity could be attributed to the strong light absorption and upconversion photoluminescence of the CQDs, and the surface plasmon resonance effect of the surface Bi also played an important role in this system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51173146), Basic Rresearch Fund of Shaanxi University of Science & Technology (2016GBJ22).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.catcom.2017.11.011.
